title: Workbook — The Holy Trinity
html:
Student Workbook

For use after class or in a group. Print this page or work in your notebook.

Section 1 — Remembering the Essentials

  1. What does it mean that the Trinity is the "central mystery of the Christian faith"? Why is it not just an advanced topic for theologians, but the foundation of everything else we believe?
  2. What are the three divine Persons, and what is the eternal relationship between them? How is the Father related to the Son, and how is the Holy Spirit related to both?
  3. What does "God is Love" (1 Jn 4:8, 16) mean for understanding the Trinity? Why is this sentence the most important key to the entire doctrine?
  4. In the baptism of Jesus (Mt 3:16-17), how do the three Persons manifest themselves simultaneously? What does this visible epiphany of the Trinity teach us about who God is?
  5. What is the difference between the Persons being "really distinct" and their sharing "one divine nature"? Why is it heresy to say either that there are three gods, or that God is one Person with three masks?

Section 2 — Deepening Your Understanding

2.1 The Progressive Revelation of the Trinity

Explain in your own words why God did not reveal himself as Trinity all at once. What "Trinitarian glimpses" can be found in the Old Testament? How does the full revelation in Jesus Christ fulfill and surpass everything that came before?

 

 

 

 

 

2.2 The Council of Nicaea and the Word That Changed Everything

What did Arius teach, and why did it matter? What does homoousios (consubstantial, "of the same substance") mean, and why was it so important to insist that the Son is "begotten, not made"? If the Son were a creature and not truly God, what would that mean for our salvation?

 

 

 

 

 

2.3 Trinitarian Analogies: Helpful but Limited

List three analogies used to help explain the Trinity (e.g., St. Patrick's shamrock, St. Augustine's lover-beloved-love, the family). For each one, identify (a) what it gets right about the Trinity, and (b) where it falls short and could lead to misunderstanding if taken too far.

 

 

 

 

 

 

2.4 The Trinitarian Missions: The Circle of Love

Draw a diagram of the "circle of love": Father → Son → Holy Spirit → us → back to the Father. Explain how the entire structure of Christian prayer — "to the Father, through the Son, in the Holy Spirit" — follows this pattern. Why is the Holy Spirit described as the "Gift" between the Father and the Son?

 

 

 

 

2.5 The Indwelling of the Trinity in the Soul

What does it mean that the Blessed Trinity "makes a home" (Jn 14:23) in the soul of every person in a state of grace? Why is mortal sin described as expelling the Trinity from the temple of the soul? How does confession restore this indwelling presence?

 

 

 

 

2.6 The Beatitudes: Happiness the Trinitarian Way

Read Matthew 5:3-10 (the Beatitudes). The world says happiness comes from wealth, power, and comfort; Jesus says happiness comes from poverty of spirit, meekness, mercy, and purity of heart. How does this reversal connect to the Trinitarian pattern of self-giving love? Pick one Beatitude and describe how it reflects the life of the Trinity.

 

 

 

 

 

Section 3 — For Reflection and Personal Writing

Answer each question honestly. There are no "correct" answers to these personal questions — only thoughtful ones.

1. If God is a communion of love from all eternity — a family of Persons who give themselves to one another — what does that tell us about the deepest meaning of our human relationships?

Think about your family, your friendships, your community. Do you tend to live these relationships as communion and mutual self-gift, or as independence and self-protection? What would it look like to model your closest relationships more deliberately on the Trinitarian pattern?

 

 

 

 

2. Is it harder for you to imagine God as solitude or as communion? Which image of God feels closer to your actual experience in prayer?

Most people instinctively imagine God as a solitary being sitting on a throne. The Christian faith says He has never been alone: He has always been Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. Does this Trinitarian image of God change the way you would approach him in prayer? Does it make God feel more distant or closer to you?

 

 

 

 

3. In what specific moment of your life have you experienced something like Trinitarian love — love that gives itself without counting the cost, that does not hold back, that generates life and joy in others?

It could be in your family, in a friendship, in an act of service, in receiving the Eucharist. Try to recall not just the general feeling but the concrete details: who was there, what was said or done, and why that moment felt different from ordinary affection.

 

 

 

 

4. The Sign of the Cross immerses us in the Trinity every time we make it. How can you live with more awareness of this mystery in your daily life?

Name one concrete, specific action you will take this week: make the Sign of the Cross slowly and deliberately each morning, pray the Glory Be with real attention, address each divine Person by name in your daily prayer, or consciously make room at your table for someone who is alone.

 

 

 

 

5. St. Paul blesses the Corinthians with "The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, the love of God, and the fellowship of the Holy Spirit be with you all" (2 Cor 13:13). What does it mean for you to live under this threefold blessing?

Grace (the gift of the Son), love (the heart of the Father), and fellowship (the communion of the Spirit) — these are the three dimensions of Trinitarian life offered to you every day. If you woke up tomorrow and truly believed these three things were yours, how would your day be different?

 

 

 

 

Section 4 — Practical Activities

A. Contemplate Rublev's Icon with Attention

Find a high-quality image of Andrei Rublev's icon of the Trinity online (search "Rublev Trinity icon high resolution"). Spend at least 10 minutes looking at it silently, without distractions. Then write: (1) What do you notice about the posture of each angel? (2) What do the three background symbols — the house, the tree, the mountain — suggest to you? (3) Why do you think there is an empty place at the front of the table? What does that empty space say to you personally?

 

 

 

 

 

 

B. Make Your Sign of the Cross a Prayer

This week, every time you make the Sign of the Cross — in the morning, before meals, entering a church, beginning and ending your prayer — do it slowly. Consciously name each Person in your heart. Touch your forehead thinking "Father, you created me"; your chest thinking "Son, you redeemed me"; each shoulder thinking "Holy Spirit, you dwell in me." At the end of the week, write a paragraph: did doing this slowly change anything about how you experienced the gesture?

 

 

 

 

 

C. Write a Trinitarian Prayer

Compose a short prayer addressed to each of the three divine Persons. The structure is simple but powerful: "Father, I thank you for ______________. Son, I thank you for ______________. Holy Spirit, I thank you for ______________." Fill each blank with something specific from your life — not something generic, but something real. Then pray this prayer aloud, addressing each Person by name.

 

 

 

 

D. Observe Trinitarian Love in Daily Life

During the coming week, keep a small journal or note on your phone. Write down three specific moments when you witnessed love that gave itself without holding back — a person who served you, a generous gesture that moved you, a moment of genuine communion. For each one, briefly note: who was involved, what happened, and why it felt like a glimpse of something bigger than ordinary kindness. At the end of the week, read your three entries and look for a pattern. What do these moments have in common?

 

 

 

 

 

 

E. Attend Mass with Trinitarian Attention

Attend a Mass this week — Sunday obligation or a weekday Mass — and go with a specific intention: notice every Trinitarian reference. Count how many times the priest prays "through our Lord Jesus Christ, your Son, who lives and reigns with you in the unity of the Holy Spirit, one God, forever and ever." Notice how the Gloria, the Creed, the Eucharistic Prayer, and the final blessing are all structured. After Mass, write: how many Trinitarian references did you count? Did seeing the liturgy through this lens change your experience of it?

 

 

 

 

 

Section 5 — Verse to Memorize

"The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, the love of God, and the fellowship of the Holy Spirit be with you all."

— 2 Corinthians 13:13

Tips for memorizing: Write it by hand three times. Say it aloud slowly, pausing at each of the three gifts: grace (gift of the Son), love (heart of the Father), fellowship (communion of the Spirit). Notice that St. Paul places these three gifts in order: the Son's work (grace), the Father's heart (love), the Spirit's fruit (fellowship). The entire Trinity is present in one sentence.

Section 6 — Prayer for This Week

Glory to the Father, and to the Son, and to the Holy Spirit,
God, one and triune,
eternal communion of love.

Father, who created me by love —
I praise you for the gift of existence,
for calling me into being not out of necessity but out of generosity.

Son, who redeemed me by your cross —
I praise you for becoming flesh, for walking my road,
for dying my death and rising to open the way home.

Holy Spirit, who dwells in me —
I praise you for every holy desire, every moment of grace,
for crying out "Abba, Father" in the depths of my heart.

Grant that I may live in communion with you and with others;
that I may never seek the solitude of selfishness,
but the joy of shared love;
that my ordinary life — my work, my rest, my relationships —
may become an image of your Trinitarian life.

Make room in me, Blessed Trinity.
Let me not keep the empty seat closed,
but welcome you fully — Father, Son, and Holy Spirit —
now and forever.
Amen.

Print Workbook
